comments.text: The smallest possible value of $\limsup \frac{\left|A \cap \{1, 2, \ldots, N \} \right|}{N^{1/2}}$ is at most $2 \varphi^{5/2} \approx 6.66$, with $\varphi$ equal to the golden ratio. I have uploaded a proof here. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

I have very marginally improved the construction to get $\left|A \cap \{1, 2, \ldots, N\} \right| < 2 \varphi^{5/2} \sqrt{N}$ for all $N \in \mathbb{N}$, even though the limit superior is still the same. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)

Nice, thanks! Looking into this problem again I discovered that there's a fair amount of literature into the $\liminf$ question (which I had unfairly overlooked initially, but have added here now). Surprisingly none of these papers appear to investigate the $\limsup$, but I haven't looked through them all in detail yet.

This method generalises to additive complement of $k$-th powers in the following way (assuming my calculations were correct). Define $$C_k(r):=\frac{k\,r^{1-\frac 1k}(r-1)^{1-\frac 1k}}{r^{1-\frac 1k}-1}$$ for a given $k$, and a parameter $r$. Then, there exists an additive complement of $k$-th powers, $A_k$ for which $$\limsup_{x\to\infty}\frac{A_k(x)}{x^{1-\frac 1k}} \,\le\, \inf_{r>1} C_k(r)$$ and it isn't hard to see that $C_k(r)$ minimises at $r=\rho_k^k$ where $\rho_k$ is the unique root of $x^{2k-1}-2x^k+1=0$ greater than $1$. For $k=3,4,5$, the RHS is approximately $7.93$, $9.02$ and $10.08$ respectively. Needless to say, $C_2(\phi^2)=2\phi^{5/2}$.
Ramama should be Ramana; couldn't help noticing. (The site has been updated to address this comment.)
